Their perspectives help ensure that youth experiences remain central to global education planning. 2. Influence International Decision-Making One of the most valuable aspects of membership is the opportunity to influence international education governance. Members provide recommendations and insights that shape ECW s work supporting children and youth affected by crises. 3. Build International Networks Organizations become part of a worldwide community of professionals and youth organizations working in: Education. Humanitarian assistance. Emergency response. Child protection. Youth leadership. Community development. These connections encourage collaboration and knowledge sharing across regions. 4. Participate in Governance Elections Members have voting rights in elections for representatives serving on ECW governance bodies. This enables organizations to participate in selecting leaders who will represent youth interests within the institution. 5. Opportunity to Seek Leadership Positions Eligible organizations may not only vote but also stand for election. During the 2026 membership cycle, organizations may seek election to represent the constituency on: High-Level Steering Group (HLSG). Executive Committee (ExCom). Successful candidates will serve a two-year mandate , representing youth voices within ECW s highest governance structures. Who Can Apply? Applications are open to eligible organizations that meet ECW s membership requirements. Applicants must be: Youth-led organizations. Student-led organizations. Not-for-profit organizations. Organizations working in education. Organizations supporting education in emergencies. Organizations working within humanitarian or crisis settings. Applicants should carefully review the official Membership Election Guidance Note for complete eligibility criteria before submitting their application. Leadership Opportunities Available Membership also provides organizations with the opportunity to play a leadership role within ECW. Representatives elected to the High-Level Steering Group or Executive Committee contribute to: Strategic planning. Governance oversight. Representation of youth interests. Policy discussions. Organizational accountability. These leadership roles help ensure that youth-led organizations have meaningful influence over ECW s future direction. Key Dates Applicants should take note of the important deadlines: Membership applications open: 6 July 2026 Deadline for membership applications and election nominations: 26 July 2026 Voting period: 3–5 August 2026 Announcement of elected representatives: 7 August 2026 How to Apply Eligible organizations should complete the official application process by following these steps: Complete the Membership Application Form. Submit the Election Nomination Form if seeking election. Download and carefully review the Membership Election Guidance Note. Ensure all eligibility requirements are met before submission. Submit the application before the deadline. APPLY HERE Why Organizations Should Apply Membership in the ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group offers far more than recognition. It provides organizations with a genuine opportunity to shape international education governance while representing the interests of young people affected by crises around the world. Participating organizations gain access to influential policy discussions, strengthen their international partnerships, contribute to global education solutions, and help ensure that youth perspectives remain central to humanitarian and development responses. For organizations seeking to expand their impact beyond national borders, strengthen their leadership profile, and influence the future of education in emergencies, this opportunity offers an exceptional platform for meaningful engagement. Visit HERE to learn more about the Education Cannot Wait (ECW) Youth and Student-led Sub-group 2026 Application Deadline 26 July 2026 Applications and election nominations must be submitted by this deadline. Conclusion The Education Cannot Wait Youth and Student-led Sub-group 2026 Membership and Election Call is a remarkable opportunity for eligible youth-led and student-led organizations committed to advancing education in emergencies and supporting crisis-affected communities. Through membership, organizations can actively participate in global policy consultations, contribute to ECW s governance, build international partnerships, vote in leadership elections, and even stand for influential positions on the High-Level Steering Group and Executive Committee. If your organization is passionate about youth leadership, education, and humanitarian action, this is an opportunity to help shape the future of global education governance while ensuring that the voices of young people remain at the heart of decision-making. Application Deadline: 26 July 2026 . For more global Fellowship opportunities, visit OFY HERE The post Education Cannot Wait (ECW) Youth and Student-led Sub-group 2026: Apply to Shape Global Education Governance and Represent Crisis-Affected Youth appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Harvard Kennedy School Shorenstein Fellows Program 2026: Prestigious Fellowship for Journalists, Technologists, Policymakers, Scholars, and Media Professionals
summary: The Shorenstein Fellows Program at the Shorenstein Center on Media, Politics and Public Policy , housed within Harvard Kennedy School , is one of the world s leading fellowship opportunities for professionals and scholars working at the intersection of media, technology, politics, and public policy. For nearly four decades, the program has served as a hub for influential thinkers, journalists, policymakers, technologists, filmmakers, creators, and researchers who are shaping how information is produced, distributed, and understood in democratic societies. Founded in 1986 , the fellowship has welcomed more than 300 distinguished fellows from around the world. These fellows have contributed groundbreaking research, influential publications, public discussions, documentaries, policy recommendations, and innovative projects that address some of the most pressing challenges affecting today s global information ecosystem. The Shorenstein Fellows Program offers selected participants the opportunity to pursue an independent project while becoming part of Harvard Kennedy School s vibrant intellectual community. Fellows collaborate with leading scholars and practitioners while producing original work that informs public debate and strengthens democratic institutions. Whether your expertise lies in journalism, artificial intelligence, technology policy, digital governance, media innovation, political communication, public leadership, or academic research, the Shorenstein Fellows Program provides a prestigious platform to expand your ideas and contribute to global conversations. About the Shorenstein Center The Shorenstein Center on Media, Politics and Public Policy is a research center at Harvard Kennedy School dedicated to studying how information shapes societies, governments, and democratic institutions. Its work focuses on understanding: Journalism and news media. Digital technology. Artificial intelligence. Public policy. Political communication. Information integrity. Democracy. Civic engagement. The evolving global information ecosystem. Through research, fellowships, publications, events, and collaborations, the Center seeks to improve the creation, distribution, and consumption of trustworthy information worldwide. About the Shorenstein Fellows Program The Shorenstein Fellows Program is designed to bring together professionals and scholars from multiple disciplines who are interested in examining critical issues surrounding media, politics, technology, and governance. The fellowship emphasizes independent scholarship while encouraging collaboration within Harvard s academic environment. Participants pursue self-directed projects that contribute meaningful insights into the changing landscape of information, democracy, and public communication. Throughout the fellowship, fellows engage with faculty, researchers, students, and fellow practitioners while sharing expertise from their own professional backgrounds. Purpose of the Fellowship The fellowship aims to: Advance public understanding of media and democracy. Support innovative research on technology and public policy. Encourage interdisciplinary collaboration. Promote thoughtful analysis of today s information ecosystem. Foster dialogue between practitioners and academics. Produce research that informs policymakers and the general public. Strengthen democratic institutions through better understanding of information flows. Areas of Focus The fellowship welcomes projects exploring a broad range of topics, including: Journalism Digital media Artificial intelligence Social media Information integrity Public policy Political communication Democracy Technology governance Election integrity Misinformation and disinformation Civic engagement Digital platforms Media ethics Public interest storytelling Documentary filmmaking Government innovation Internet governance Freedom of expression Data and society Interdisciplinary proposals that combine multiple fields are particularly encouraged. What Fellows Do During the fellowship, participants pursue an independent project while actively engaging with the Shorenstein Center community. Projects may include: Academic research Policy papers Opinion articles Essays Investigative journalism Reports Webinars Documentary storytelling Multimedia projects Public presentations Books or book chapters Digital publications The fellowship encourages work that translates complex ideas into accessible content for both policymakers and the general public. Contribution to the Research Community Beyond completing their own projects, fellows contribute to the broader intellectual life of the Center by: Participating in discussions. Sharing expertise with faculty and students. Collaborating across disciplines. Presenting research findings. Engaging in seminars and public events. Supporting dialogue around contemporary media and policy challenges. This collaborative environment allows fellows to benefit from diverse perspectives while expanding the impact of their own work. Distinguished Fellowship Community Since its establishment, the Shorenstein Fellows Program has hosted an impressive community of professionals representing journalism, technology, government, academia, filmmaking, and policy. The Center has welcomed over 300 fellows since 1986, reflecting its longstanding commitment to fostering thought leadership across multiple sectors. Recent fellows have included: Journalists Documentary filmmakers Artificial intelligence experts Technology leaders Public policy specialists Government officials Academic researchers Digital innovation experts Media executives This diverse fellowship community creates valuable opportunities for networking, collaboration, and intellectual exchange. Research and Publications Fellows contribute to an extensive body of work addressing today s most important challenges surrounding media and democracy.
